projects.title: Unit 8 Project — Statistics in Action
projects.desc: Capstone: choose the measure of center and spread that best fits your data's distribution.

projects.title: Distribution Detective
projects.desc: Decide which measure of center best describes a data set based on its shape (6.SP.5d).

projects.title: Data Studio Master
projects.desc: Compare mean, median, and spread to pick the most appropriate summary measure.

title: Appropriate Measures
contentObjective: I can choose the best measure of center for a data set based on its shape.
languageObjective: I can explain my choice using the words mean, median, outlier, and skewed.

turnAndTalk.question: A top scorer's points were 22, 24, 20, 25, 23, 21, 58. The mean is 27.6 but the median is 23. Which should the league report as the 'typical' game, and why?
turnAndTalk.kernel: An outlier can pull the mean away from what is typical, making the median a better choice.

stems.en: The league should report the ___ because ___.
stems.es: La liga debería reportar la ___ porque ___.

stems.en: The 58-point game is an ___ that affects the mean.
stems.es: El juego de 58 puntos es un ___ que afecta la media.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students choose the median (23) and identify 58 as an outlier that pulls the mean up to 27.6, making it unrepresentative.
turnAndTalk.extend: Justify: by how much does removing the 58-point game change the mean? What does that show about outliers?
extendStems: Without the 58, the mean drops to about ___ because ___.
This shows the median is ___ to outliers while the mean is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: As you sort scenarios into 'Use Mean' or 'Use Median,' what clue tells you a data set needs the median instead of the mean?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Data with an outlier or a skewed shape is better summarized by the median.

stems.en: I use the median when the data has ___.
stems.es: Uso la mediana cuando los datos tienen ___.

stems.en: I use the mean when the data is ___.
stems.es: Uso la media cuando los datos son ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer says the presence of an outlier or skew signals the median, while symmetric data with no outliers fits the mean.
turnAndTalk.extend: Compare: give one sports example where the mean is the better choice and one where the median is. Justify each.
extendStems: The mean is better for ___ because ___.
The median is better for ___ because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A reporter writes 'the average playoff ticket is $85,' but prices are $45, $50, $48, $52, $55, $260. Is $85 a fair 'typical' price? What measure should the reporter use?
turnAndTalk.kernel: When one extreme value skews the data, the median represents the typical case more fairly.

stems.en: $85 is / is not fair because the $260 ticket ___.
stems.es: $85 es / no es justo porque el boleto de $260 ___.

stems.en: The reporter should use the ___ because ___.
stems.es: El reportero debería usar la ___ porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain $85 is misleading because the $260 courtside seat skews the mean upward, and the median (~$51) better reflects a typical ticket.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique: could the reporter be using the mean ON PURPOSE to make tickets sound pricier? Argue whether that is honest.
extendStems: Using the mean here could be misleading because ___.
An honest report would show ___ so readers understand ___.

turnAndTalk.question: For the data set 15, 18, 16, 17, 15, 72, which measure of center best represents the data, and why?
turnAndTalk.kernel: An extreme value makes the median the more representative measure of center.

stems.en: The ___ best represents the data because ___.
stems.es: La ___ representa mejor los datos porque ___.

stems.en: The value ___ is an outlier that distorts the mean.
stems.es: El valor ___ es un valor atípico que distorsiona la media.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students choose the median (~16.5), identify 72 as the outlier, and explain it would inflate the mean.
turnAndTalk.extend: Generalize: state a rule for deciding between the mean and the median for any data set.
extendStems: Choose the median when the data has ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Appropriate Measures, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

stems.en: I use the median when the data has ___.
stems.es: Uso la mediana cuando los datos tienen ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.SP.5d — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: The average. Add all the numbers, then divide by how many there are.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El promedio. Suma todos los números y divide entre cuántos hay.
vocabulary.cloze: The average of a data set is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Tổng các giá trị chia cho số lượng các giá trị trong tập dữ liệu.
vocabulary.definitionAr: مجموع القيم مقسوماً على عددها في مجموعة البيانات.

vocabulary.definition: The middle number when you put them in order.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número del medio cuando los pones en orden.
vocabulary.cloze: The middle value when data is in order, often best when there is an outlier, is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Số nằm ở giữa khi tập dữ liệu được sắp xếp theo thứ tự.
vocabulary.definitionAr: الرقم الأوسط في مجموعة البيانات بعد ترتيبها تصاعدياً.

vocabulary.definition: A number that is much bigger or smaller than the rest.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número mucho mayor o menor que los demás.
vocabulary.visual: Data: 12, 14, 13, 15, 45 → 45 is far from the cluster, so it is an outlier
vocabulary.cloze: A value far from the rest of the data, which can pull the mean, is an ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Điểm dữ liệu khác biệt rất nhiều so với phần còn lại.
vocabulary.definitionAr: قيمة بيانات تختلف اختلافاً كبيراً عن بقية القيم.

vocabulary.definition: When most data sits on one side with a tail on the other.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Cuando la mayoría de los datos está de un lado con una cola del otro.
vocabulary.visual: Scores: 5, 6, 7, 8, 8, 35 → most scores are low, but 35 creates a tail to the right (skewed right)
vocabulary.cloze: Data that is bunched on one side with a tail on the other is ___.

examples.text: Most values low with a few very high values
examples.why: The data has a tail, so it is skewed.

examples.text: Values spread evenly around the center

vocabulary.definitionVi: Phân bố dữ liệu có một bên kéo dài hơn bên kia.
vocabulary.definitionAr: توزيع للبيانات يكون فيه أحد الجانبين أطول من الآخر.

vocabulary.definition: How the data looks: where it sits and how spread out it is.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Cómo se ven los datos: dónde están y qué tan separados están.
vocabulary.visual: Symmetric = even on both sides. Skewed = bunched on one side with a tail
vocabulary.cloze: The way data values are spread out is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Mô tả cách thức các điểm dữ liệu trải rộng trên một tập dữ liệu.
vocabulary.definitionAr: وصف لكيفية انتشار نقاط البيانات في مجموعة البيانات.

vocabulary.definition: How spread out the numbers are.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Qué tan separados están los números.
vocabulary.cloze: How much the data values differ is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Mức độ khác biệt hoặc phân tán của dữ liệu.
vocabulary.definitionAr: مدى انتشار أو اختلاف القيم في مجموعة البيانات.

launch.narrative: The league is creating awards for the season. For the scoring title, they need to pick the best measure of a typical game. Here are the top scorer's points per game: 22, 24, 20, 25, 23, 21, 58. That 58-point game was a record-breaker! Should the league report the mean (27.6) or the median (23) as the player's typical scoring?
launch.contextImage: Trophy display case with player stats and a giant 58 highlighted
noticePrompts: How does the 58-point game compare to the other scores?
What is the mean? What is the median?
Which measure is closer to what this player usually scores?
wonderPrompts: When does an outlier make the mean misleading?
Is there a rule for when to use mean vs. median?

visual.title: Top scorer — points per game
visual.caption: Notice the lone 58 (an outlier). It pulls the mean up to 27.6, while the median stays at 23.

conceptIntro.heading: Should I use the mean or the median?
conceptIntro.intro: The mean and the median both describe the center of a data set, but one fits better depending on the shape. When the data has an outlier (a value far from the rest), the median is usually the better choice.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: My data, the top scorer's points: 22, 24, 20, 25, 23, 21, 58. I notice 58 is much bigger than the rest, so it is an outlier.
Median: in order they are 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 58. The middle (4th) value is 23.
The mean (27.6) is higher than 6 of the 7 games because 58 pulled it up. So the median (23) better shows a typical game.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Data set A: 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. Are there any outliers? If not, which measure should we use, mean or median?
Data set B: 4, 5, 6, 7, 40. Which value is an outlier here?
For data set B, would the outlier pull the mean up or down? So which measure best shows a typical value?

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next, you will sort sports scenarios into "Use Mean" or "Use Median."
For each one, look for an outlier or a skewed shape to decide.

explore.label: Sort each sports data scenario into the correct category: Use Mean or Use Median.

cards.text: Basketball: Points per game — 18, 20, 19, 22, 21, 20 (no outliers, symmetric)

discourse.prompt: What pattern do you notice about when median is the better choice?
discourse.sentenceFrame: The median is better when the data has ___ because the mean gets pulled toward ___. The mean is better when the data is ___ because ___.

optionalActivity.name: Best Measure Decision Cards
optionalActivity.intro: You are the team's data analyst — read each scouting question and pick the measure that answers it best.

optional.stem: A scout records points per game: 14, 16, 15, 17, 16, 58. To report a TYPICAL game, which measure of center is best?
choices: Median, because 58 is an outlier that pulls the mean up
Mean, because it uses every game
Range, because it shows the highest game
Mode, because 16 appears twice
optional.explanation: The 58-point game is an outlier. It pulls the mean above 5 of the 6 games, so the median (16) better shows a typical game.

optional.stem: The equipment manager needs to order the MOST COMMON jersey size from this list: M, L, M, S, M, L, M. Which measure answers the question?
choices: Mode, because it names the value that appears most
Mean, because it averages the sizes
Median, because it finds the middle size
Range, because it shows the size gap
optional.explanation: The mode is the value that shows up most often. Here size M appears 4 times, so the mode (M) tells the manager what to order most.

optional.stem: A coach wants to know how SPREAD OUT the team's free-throw makes are: 6, 7, 6, 8, 18. Which measure of spread is most affected by the lone 18?
choices: Range, because it is the highest minus the lowest value
Median, because it is in the middle
Mode, because it is the most common
Mean absolute deviation only, never the range
optional.explanation: Range = max − min = 18 − 6 = 12. Because it uses only the two most extreme values, one outlier (18) changes the range a lot.

optional.label: Match each scouting question to the measure that answers it.

pairs.term: What was a typical game when one score is a record-breaking outlier?
Which shoe size do most players wear?
On a symmetric, outlier-free set, what is the average score?
How far apart are the highest and lowest sprint times?

optional.instructions: Sort each measure into whether it describes the CENTER of the data or the SPREAD of the data.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The Analyst's Wrong Measure

workedExample.label: Data
workedExample.work: A player's points per game: 19, 21, 20, 22, 23, 2 (the 2 was a game cut short by an early ejection).

workedExample.label: Analyst's choice
workedExample.work: The analyst reports the mean (17.8) as the player's typical scoring because it uses all the data.

optional.correctWork: The analyst should report the median (20.5), not the mean. The score of 2 is an outlier from the cut-short game. It pulls the mean down to 17.8, which is lower than 5 of the 6 games, so it does not represent a typical performance. The median (20.5) is resistant to the outlier and better describes a typical game.
hints: Look at the value 2. Does it fit with the rest of the player's scores, or is it an outlier?
The mean (17.8) is lower than 5 of the 6 games. A good 'typical' value should sit among most of the data, like the median (20.5) does.

approaching.stem: A gymnast's scores are: 8.5, 8.8, 8.7, 8.6, 8.9. There are no outliers. Which measure best represents a typical score?
choices: Mean
Median
Neither — the data is too small
Both are equally bad
approaching.explanation: The data is symmetric with no outliers, so the mean (8.7) best represents the typical score.

approaching.stem: A runner's mile times are: 7:10, 7:15, 7:12, 7:20, 12:00. The 12:00 was due to a cramp. Which measure best represents a typical mile?
choices: Median — the outlier 12:00 pulls the mean too high
Mean — it uses all the data
Mode — it shows the most common time
Neither measure works
approaching.explanation: The 12:00 is an outlier that pulls the mean up. The median (7:15) better represents the runner's typical time.

approaching.stem: Match each data description with the best measure of center.
Data set: 5, 6, 7, 7, 8, 50. The mean is about 13.8 and the median is 7. Which better represents the typical value?
choices: Median (7) — the outlier 50 inflates the mean
Mean (13.8) — it includes all values
Both are equally good
Neither works for this data
approaching.explanation: The mean (13.8) is higher than 5 of the 6 values because the outlier 50 pulls it up. The median (7) better represents a typical value.

approaching.instructions: Sort each data feature into whether it suggests using the mean or the median.

categories.label: Use Median
items: Data has one or more outliers
Data is skewed to one side
One extreme value pulls the average

hints: The mean is sensitive to extreme values — outliers pull it up or down.
The median stays in the middle regardless of how extreme the highest or lowest values are.
approaching.label: Sort each feature by which measure of center it supports.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Appropriate Measures.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

onLevel.stem: A baseball player's batting averages over 6 seasons are: .280, .295, .290, .285, .300, .110. The .110 was an injury-shortened season. Which measure better represents the player's typical batting average?
choices: Median, because the .110 outlier pulls the mean down
Mean, because it uses all the data
Mean, because .110 is a real season
Neither measure works
onLevel.explanation: The .110 is an outlier that pulls the mean down to .260. The median (.2875) better represents typical performance because it isn't affected by the extreme value.

editableCells.answer: Median (72 pulls the mean to 27.6, higher than 4 of 5 values)

onLevel.stem: Match the reason with whether to use mean or median.

pairs.left: Data is symmetric and has no extreme values
Data has one or more outliers
Data is skewed to one side
All values are clustered close together

onLevel.label: Data: 10, 12, 11, 13, 50. Place the mean (19.2) and median (12) on the number line. Notice how far apart they are because of the outlier.
hints: The mean (19.2) is pulled toward the outlier 50. The median (12) stays near the cluster of data.
When the mean and median are far apart, that is a sign of skewed data or an outlier.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Appropriate Measures.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical.

extending.title: Find the Reasoning Error

workedExample.label: Conclusion
workedExample.work: The mean (28.2) is the best measure because it uses all the data.

extending.correctWork: The median (32.5) is the better measure. The value 5 is an outlier (the player was injured that game) that pulls the mean down to 28.2, which is lower than any of the player's healthy games. The median (32.5) better represents typical minutes played.
hints: Look at the value 5. Does it fit with the rest of the data?
The mean (28.2) is lower than 5 of the 6 values. Does that seem like a 'typical' game?

extending.prompt: A local newspaper reports that the average home price in a neighborhood is $450,000. The actual prices are: $180K, $200K, $190K, $210K, $195K, $1,725K. Is the reporter's claim misleading? Calculate the mean and median and explain which better represents a 'typical' home price.
extending.sentenceFrame: Mean = ___. Median = ___. The reporter's claim is ___ because the outlier ($1,725K) pulls the mean ___. The ___ (about $___) better represents a typical home price because ___.

extending.instructions: Sort each real-world situation into whether the mean or median is more commonly reported.

categories.label: Mean is typically reported
items: Average test score for a class (no outliers)

categories.label: Median is typically reported
items: Median household income in a city
Median home price in a neighborhood

hints: Income and home prices often have extreme outliers (billionaires, mansions), so median is used.
Temperature and test scores are often symmetric, so mean works well.
extending.label: Sort by which measure is typically used in real life.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Appropriate Measures is skipping the key idea: "Use the mean for data with no outliers; use the median when an outlier or a skewed shape would pull the mean away from typical." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A sports reporter writes: 'The average ticket price for the playoffs is $85.' The actual prices are: $45, $50, $48, $52, $55, $260. The $260 ticket is a courtside seat.
connect.promptQuestion: Is $85 a fair way to describe a 'typical' ticket price? What measure should the reporter use?
connect.prompt: The reporter used the ___, which is ___. A better measure would be the ___ (about ___) because ___. The courtside seat at $260 is an ___ that pulls the mean ___.

exitTicket.stem: Data set: 15, 18, 16, 17, 15, 72. Which measure of center best represents the data?
choices: Median, because 72 is an outlier
Mean, because it uses all values
Mean, because it is always best
Neither works for this data
exitTicket.explanation: The value 72 is an outlier. The mean (25.5) is pulled high by 72 and doesn't represent the typical values. The median (16.5) is a better measure of center.
